affected_groups: young students facing selection at ages 10-14
content.decision_question: Does the psychological benefit of fewer, later sorting points from delaying or capping early selection outweigh the transition-related cognitive load and the risk that displaced demand concentrates psychological pressure at remaining entry points?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can measure self-concept, motivation, and anxiety outcomes before and after delayed-tracking reforms, and can track whether competitive intensity increases at remaining entry points. It cannot determine how much weight to give protecting long-term psychological development versus minimizing near-term disruption for currently enrolled cohorts, since this involves competing considerations about whose welfare (current versus future students) should take priority.
content.tension: Delaying selection to age 14 reduces the number of high-stakes sorting junctures implicated in big-fish-little-pond and goal-orientation dynamics, potentially benefiting self-concept and motivation broadly, but the transition process itself may introduce short-term cognitive load and uncertainty, and partial reforms that retain some early sorting points may concentrate rather than dissipate psychological pressure at the remaining transition.
content.title: Reference-group and self-concept effects: delaying selection versus preserving current stratification
value_poles: protecting long-run psychological development through delayed high-stakes sorting
avoiding short-term disruption and uncertainty for students during transition
